workflow_directives: Read the [ROADMAP](./ROADMAP.md) before touching anything.
Drive the real harness for QA and write evidence to disk.
For changes under `packages/omo-opencode/`, always run the `opencode-qa` skill.
Isolate all `opencode` QA runs in isolated XDG sandboxes with temporary directories.

For changes under `packages/omo-codex/`, always run the `codex-qa` skill.
Run the `codex-qa` skill in strict isolation with an isolated `CODEX_HOME` and local mock model.
Run the hermetic unit gate `bun run test:codex`.
Record all QA artifacts (before/after state, isolation proof, commands, outputs, tmux captures) under `.omo/evidence/<YYYYMMDD>-<short-slug>/`.
Unless explicitly instructed otherwise, deliver changes using the `work-with-pr` skill.
The `work-with-pr` skill operates in an isolated git worktree, includes evidence-bound manual QA, opens a detailed English PR, runs verification, and merges.
Resolve merge conflicts using the `smart-rebase` skill, then re-run scoped QA.
Land pull requests with merge commits using `gh pr merge <number> --merge --delete-branch`.
Run CI tests with plain `bun test` in one process; no sharding.
Ensure `test-setup.ts` is preloaded via `bunfig.toml` to reset session/cache state between tests.
The runtime environment must be Bun only (1.3.12 in CI); do not use npm/yarn/pnpm (with specific exceptions for vendored Node-targeted packages).
CI automatically commits schema changes on `master` pushes and creates a draft 'next' release on `dev` pushes.
unique_innovations: Mandatory QA with recorded evidence: The system explicitly states that 'no evidence file == no QA == no commit == no push,' making the proof of testing a hard gate for any code change and directly addressing AI agent overconfidence.
Isolated QA environments: Requires `opencode` and `codex` QA to run in isolated XDG sandboxes, preventing pollution of real user environments and ensuring test integrity/reproducibility.

Agent-driven PR workflow (`work-with-pr` skill): Automates the entire pull request lifecycle, including isolated worktree creation, evidence-bound manual QA, PR drafting, and merging, effectively guiding the agent through complex development workflows.
Agent-driven conflict resolution (`smart-rebase` skill): Provides an agent-native mechanism for resolving merge conflicts, which is a common challenge for autonomous agents.

Hashline edit + read pairing: All `Read` tool outputs are tagged with `LINE#ID` content hashes; `hashline_edit` validates this hash before applying changes, preventing edits based on stale file content and mitigating edit-related hallucinations.

Runtime architectural invariant enforcement via `rules-injector` hook: The system actively reads `.omo/rules/*.md` and enforces architecture rules at runtime by injecting them into the agent's context.

Test discipline meta-audits: Dedicated tests (e.g., `prompt-async-route-audit.test.ts`) use the TypeScript compiler API to parse the entire codebase and fail the test suite if architectural invariants (like proper `session.promptAsync` usage) are violated, creating a robust self-checking mechanism for code quality and agent behavior.
Comment checker hook: Blocks 'AI slop comment patterns' to maintain high code quality and prevent generic, low-value comments often generated by AI.
First-prompt watchdog: Detects subagent sessions that produce no progress within 90 seconds and triggers fallback or abort actions, preventing agents from getting stuck in infinite loops.

Strict `prompt-async-gate.ts` for internal message injection: Enforces a critical shared gate for all `session.prompt` / `session.promptAsync` calls, with explicit forbidden patterns and required semantics (e.g., session reservation, post-dispatch hold) to prevent duplicate messages, state corruption, and agent-induced chaos in shared session state.

core_constraints: Never manually modify generated files; always use `make reset` to update them.
Do not hardcode component versions or container image tags; these are managed via Makefile variables and environment variables.
Be aware that admission webhooks are disabled during local development (`make run`); do not assume webhook behavior in this mode.
formatting_rules: All Go code must be formatted using `make fmt` (which leverages `goimports` and `gofmt`).
Ensure code quality and style compliance by running `make lint`.
workflow_directives: To build the operator binary, use `make build`.
To run unit tests, execute `make test`.
To generate or update all necessary code (CRDs, manifests, etc.), run `make reset`.
For local development without deploying to a cluster, use `make run` (note that webhooks are disabled in this mode).
To prepare a development environment with `cert-manager` and `MinIO`, use `make cert-manager deploy-minio`.
To build a custom Docker image and deploy it to a connected Kubernetes cluster, set `IMG_PREFIX` and `OPERATOR_VERSION` environment variables and run `make docker-build docker-push deploy reset`.

To build and deploy a custom image to an OpenShift cluster, set `IMG_PREFIX`, `OPERATOR_VERSION`, `BUNDLE_VARIANT=openshift`, and `OPERATOR_NAMESPACE` then run `make build docker-build docker-push bundle bundle-build bundle-push olm-deploy reset`.

To upgrade an operator in an OpenShift cluster with a custom image, use the appropriate `IMG_PREFIX`, `OPERATOR_VERSION`, `BUNDLE_VARIANT`, and `OPERATOR_NAMESPACE` variables with `make build docker-build docker-push bundle bundle-build bundle-push olm-upgrade reset`.
To run end-to-end tests across Kubernetes and OpenShift, use `make e2e`.
To run upgrade-specific end-to-end tests, use `make e2e-upgrade`.
To run OpenShift-specific end-to-end tests, use `make e2e-openshift` (be aware these tests can be long-running).
To run a specific end-to-end test suite, use `chainsaw test --test-dir ./tests/e2e/<suite_name>`.
To run a specific end-to-end test and keep deployed objects for debugging, add `--skip-delete` to the `chainsaw test` command.
To run a single Go unit test, use `go test ./<package_path>/... -run <TestNameRegex>`.
unique_innovations: The use of `make reset` as a single, atomic command to update all generated files is crucial for maintaining consistency and preventing manual errors.

Explicitly detailing how component versions are managed in the Makefile and container images via environment variables guides the AI to the correct source of truth, preventing hallucinated or hardcoded values.
The distinction between local development (`make run` with webhooks disabled) and full deployment helps the AI understand different operational contexts and their implications.
Providing commands for running single end-to-end tests and unit tests, especially with options like `--skip-delete` for debugging, enables more targeted and efficient problem-solving by the AI.
The clear listing of core components, directory structure, and dependencies provides comprehensive contextual awareness for the AI, reducing ambiguity during code analysis and generation tasks.

workflow_directives: Use `yarn install` to install dependencies, `yarn add <package>` to add new dependencies, and `yarn add -D <package>` for dev dependencies

Each commit should be standalone, build successfully, and contribute a clear improvement to the codebase

Mark Pull Requests that have dependencies on unmerged features ('future commits') as draft until all dependencies are merged
For addressing PR feedback, use `git commit --fixup <hash>` and then squash these commits using `git rebase -i --autosquash` to maintain a clean, linear history
Manage branch dependencies by creating pseudo-merge commits with 'future! branch-name' markers for features depending on unmerged work
When base branches of dependent features change, update dependent branches using `git rebase --onto`
Remove 'future commits' markers by rebasing the branch onto the main development branch once all dependencies are merged
unique_innovations: Implement a robust branch dependency management system using 'future! branch-name' pseudo-merge commits and precise `git rebase --onto` instructions to clearly signal and manage features dependent on other unmerged work, preventing premature integration and simplifying complex feature development flows.

Enforce a clean and meaningful Git history by mandating the use of `git commit --fixup <hash>` for iterative changes during PR review, followed by `git rebase -i --autosquash` to automatically squash these feedback commits into their original base commits, ensuring a streamlined and understandable commit log.

core_constraints: Do not manually publish or create tags; the automated workflow handles this.
Avoid introducing any regressions or breaking changes when implementing fixes or merging pull requests.

formatting_rules: Use clear and understandable commit messages.
Pull Request titles for releases must follow the format: `(release) vX.Y.Z`.
Changelogs must adhere to a categorized format, including a high-level summary and emoji-prefixed headers (e.g., `:rocket: New Feature`).
Changelog entries must use the ISO date format `YYYY-MM-DD`.
Changelog entries must retain the original commit message format (e.g., `feat:`, `fix:`).
Only include category sections in the changelog that have actual changes.
List committers alphabetically at the end of each changelog entry.
Adhere to specified emoji-based category headers and their corresponding commit prefixes (e.g., `:rocket:` for `feat`, `:bug:` for `fix`, `:house:` for `refactor`).
workflow_directives: Run `yarn lint` and `yarn test` before committing any code changes, except for documentation or comment-only updates.
Follow the developer quick start: `git clone`, `cd docusaurus-openapi-docs`, `yarn`, `yarn build-packages`, `yarn watch:demo`.
Triage issues to determine if they are questions, documentation requests, or bugs, and investigate carefully to confirm the problem's origin.
When reviewing Pull Requests, first assess if the contribution updates documentation, fixes a bug, or adds/enhances a feature.
Ensure Pull Request proposals fit the project's scope and do not duplicate existing documentation or functionality.

For releases, follow a 4-step process: 1) Bump the version using `yarn release:version <bump>` (choosing from patch, minor, major, preminor, prerelease, graduate, betamajor, betapatch). 2) Generate the changelog using `yarn release:changelog`, then copy and prepend its output to `CHANGELOG.md` following the specified format. 3) Update version references in `README.md` files and other relevant documentation. 4) Commit the changes with the message `Prepare release vX.Y.Z` and open a Pull Request targeting `main`.
Understand that releases are automatically published by a GitHub Action (`release.yaml`) upon merging a release PR to `main`, which handles npm publishing and git tagging.

For beta releases, use `betamajor` or `betapatch` version bumps, generate the changelog, and merge to a designated beta branch (e.g., `v3.0.0`), where a separate workflow (`release-beta.yaml`) publishes with the `beta` npm tag.
Provide a clear summary during PR review indicating readiness to merge or required changes.
unique_innovations: Provides a highly detailed, multi-step, and automated release process using Lerna and GitHub Actions, covering standard, pre-release, and beta workflows.
Implements a prescriptive changelog format with emoji-based category headers and corresponding commit prefixes, ensuring consistency and clarity.
Includes a dedicated 'Troubleshooting Releases' section that proactively addresses common issues during the automated release process with specific solutions.
Explicitly instructs issue and pull request reviewers to check for regressions and breaking changes, fostering project stability and quality.
Defines a comprehensive set of semantic versioning bump types, including specific ones for pre-releases and beta versions, for precise version control.

core_constraints: MADNESS targets POSIX only (Linux, macOS on x86-64, ARM64, and IBM Power); Windows is not supported.
Do not add `#ifdef _WIN32` branches, path handling that assumes backslashes, or dependencies that are Windows-only.
BLAS implementations must be sequential (single-threaded) to avoid oversubscription issues.
Do not casually flip `BUILD_SHARED_LIBS` due to assumptions about ASLR.
Any new code reaching for MPI must go through `SafeMPI` / `World::mpi`.
Do not append `Co-Authored-By: …` trailers crediting AI tooling in commit messages.
`madness::initialize` must bracket all `World` / MPI use, and `madness::finalize` mirrors it; creating a `World` or submitting tasks outside this window is undefined.
`World` lifetimes must nest (destroy in reverse creation order).
`WorldObject` and `WorldContainer` require collective construction (every rank in the enclosing `World` must create the same object in the same order).
Active-message handlers (`World::am`) run on runtime threads and must not block waiting on further tasks or collective ops; post continuations instead.
MPI is initialized at `MPI_THREAD_MULTIPLE`.
Never call MPI blocking collectives (e.g., `MPI_Barrier`, `MPI_Allreduce`); use MADNESS global ops (`World::gop`).
Outside tasks and the main thread, assume nothing in the runtime is thread-safe unless the header documents it.
`FunctionDefaults<NDIM>` must match between save and load (same cell, `k`, `thresh`, and boundary conditions); there is no migration path.
Do not mutate `Function`s from arbitrary user threads (only tasks or the main thread).
`SeparatedConvolution` operators are tied to a specific `k`, box size, and threshold; reusing one across `Function`s built with different defaults leads to undefined results.
Do not use generic target names (e.g., `check`, `doc`, `unittests`, `applications`) in CMake to avoid collisions with parent projects.
formatting_rules: Write plain commit messages describing the change; do not append `Co-Authored-By: …` trailers crediting AI tooling.
Adhere to the `.clang-tidy` configuration checked into the repository.
Choose error handling macros (`MADNESS_CHECK`, `MADNESS_ASSERT`, `MADNESS_EXCEPTION`, `MADNESS_ASSERT_NOEXCEPT`) based on the *intent* (why checking) rather than what is short to type.
Prefer explicit `World&` parameters over `World::get_default()` in any new API.
Prefer the vectorized forms in `src/madness/mra/vmra.h` (e.g., `std::vector<Function>` arithmetic) over hand-written loops over individual `Function`s.
workflow_directives: Prefer reusing an existing out-of-tree build directory over creating a new one from scratch.
Use CMake scope flags like `-DMADNESS_BUILD_MADWORLD_ONLY=ON` or `-DMADNESS_BUILD_LIBRARIES_ONLY=ON` to substantially reduce rebuild time for focused iteration.
Tests live next to the sources they exercise and are registered through `add_unittests`.
Run a subset of tests using `ctest -L short -R "madness/test/mra"` or by invoking test binaries directly.
Perform smoke tests for `testsuite` and `moldft` as described in the documentation.
Run `clang-tidy` against changed translation units before finishing non-trivial C++ edits.
Maintain CMake invariants for both installed package consumption and subproject consumption (via `FetchContent` / `add_subdirectory`).
Use the `add_mad_*` wrappers (`add_mad_library`, `add_mad_hdr_library`, `add_mad_executable`), not raw `add_library` / `add_executable`, to apply project-wide include paths and install rules.
Ensure targets are consumable from both the install tree and the build tree, preserving this via `install(EXPORT)` / `export(EXPORT)` and generator expressions.
Optional dependencies should follow the `FindOrFetch*` pattern (try `find_package` first, fall back to `FetchContent`).
Add new warning-related flags to the `madness_internal_warnings` INTERFACE target, not to individual targets.

During PR review, actively check for raw MPI calls, blocking MPI collectives, implicit default World usage, non-collective construction, threaded-BLAS assumptions, `Function` mutation from user threads, checkpoint-format breaks, and tensor-layer changes without `GENTENSOR` coverage.
unique_innovations: Explicitly states that 'the code is the source of truth' and to flag discrepancies in the `AGENTS.md` file itself.

Provides CMake options (`-DMADNESS_ASSUMES_ASLR_DISABLED=ON`, `-DENABLE_NEVER_SPIN=ON`, `-DENABLE_GENTENSOR=ON`) to handle specific deployment environments or conditional code paths (e.g., ASLR-less systems, VMs, 6D tensor work).
Uses `stubmpi.h` to allow compiling MPI-related code even when `-DENABLE_MPI=OFF` is set, avoiding compilation errors.
Provides a specific environment variable workaround (`HWLOC_COMPONENTS=darwin,no_os,-opencl`) for Homebrew OpenMPI issues on Apple Silicon.
Uses a custom CMake macro `add_custom_target_subproject` to define subproject-scoped targets (e.g., `<name>-madness`) that integrate smoothly with parent projects.
Leverages `$<BUILD_INTERFACE:…>` / `$<INSTALL_INTERFACE:…>` generator expressions in CMake for robustly handling targets consumable from both build and install trees.
Sets `CMAKE_FIND_NO_INSTALL_PREFIX=ON` as a safety net to prevent `find_package` from picking up stale installed copies during development.

Organizes public link targets into component libraries (e.g., `MADworld`) and per-component object libraries (e.g., `MADworld-obj`) for lean downstream dependencies and faster isolated component builds.
Manages internal warning policy on an `INTERFACE` target (`madness_internal_warnings`) linked `PRIVATE-ly` to object libraries, preventing warning flags from propagating to downstream consumers.
Mandates `SafeMPI` / `World::mpi` for all MPI calls, providing automated error checking, optional serialization fallback for non-thread-safe MPI, and compatibility with `ENABLE_MPI=OFF` builds.
Directs agents to `madness::archive` as the public serialization extension point, teaching `Future`, `WorldContainer`, and AM payloads to transport custom types.
Provides `MemoryMeasurer::measure_and_print` and CMake flags (`-DENABLE_MEM_STATS`, `-DENABLE_TENSOR_INSTANCE_COUNT`) for memory profiling and leak hunting.
Includes a dedicated 'Reviewing a PR' section that identifies specific, subtle regressions CI might miss, guiding agents in expert-level code review.
Warns that `Function<T,NDIM>` is a 'shallow-copy handle' with Python-like reference semantics, explicitly stating `Function b = a;` shares the implementation.

project_type: Monorepo for a web application suite, including backend API services (Go, GraphQL, HTTP), frontend applications (Vue 3, Nuxt 3), and shared libraries.
core_constraints: Adhere strictly to these guidelines to maintain code consistency and quality.
Always prefer CodeGraph MCP over manual file searching when CodeGraph is configured.
Avoid searching the internet for library documentation; always use Context7 MCP if available for up-to-date information.
Do not create new delete confirmation components; import and use the existing standardized component.
DO NOT use the `<Form>` component provided by `vee-validate`; bind submission logic to a native HTML `<form>` element's `@submit` event instead.
Always use `lucide-vue-next` for icons; using another library or local SVG is a rare exception only if an icon is absolutely not available in Lucide.
All styling must be done using Tailwind CSS utility classes directly in the `<template>` block.
Avoid writing custom CSS in `<style>` blocks unless absolutely necessary for complex, non-reusable scenarios.
Adhere strictly to the project's `tailwind.config.js` for colors, spacing, and sizing.
Do not use arbitrary hex codes or default Tailwind colors if custom ones are defined in the project configuration.
Do not use arbitrary spacing or sizing values (e.g., `p-[15px]`); use the defined spacing scale (e.g., `p-4`, `m-8`, `w-32`).
In the Go backend, NEVER use GORM or other ORMs; always use `pgx` implementations for database access.
In Go, do not create separate files for models within repositories; include `isNil` property, `IsNil` method, and `var Nil = &Model{}` directly in the repository's model definition.
formatting_rules: In Vue.js, always use the Composition API.
All Vue Single File Components (SFCs) must use the `<script setup lang="ts">` syntax.
Do not use the `setup()` function within the `export default` block in Vue SFCs.
Use `defineProps`, `defineEmits`, and `defineSlots` with explicit TypeScript types for clear, type-safe component interfaces.
Use Vue's new `defineModel` syntax instead of `emit('update:modelValue', value)` for two-way binding.
Always include the `.vue` file extension when importing Vue components.
For forms, use `zod` to define validation schemas.
For forms, use the `@vee-validate/zod` library to connect `zod` schemas to `vee-validate`.
For forms, use the `useForm` hook from `vee-validate`.
For forms, for standard text inputs, use `v-slot="{ componentField }"` on the field wrapper and bind `v-bind="componentField"` to the input element.
For forms, for switches, checkboxes, and custom toggle components, use `v-slot="{ value, handleChange }"` to manage state.
For forms, always include the `FormMessage` component immediately after a form field to display validation errors.
For iconography in the Dashboard (`frontend`), import icons by name from `lucide-vue-next` (e.g., `import { User } from 'lucide-vue-next';`).
For iconography in the Web (`web`), use the `<Icon />` Nuxt component and pass the name of the icon as `name="lucide:icon-name"`.
In Go backend, follow standard Go formatting (`gofmt`/`goimports`).
In Go, repositories should return entities defined in `libs/entities/{entity_name}/entity.go`.
In Go, always create an entity mapper when creating new services (e.g., in `api-gql`).
In Go, ensure data flow: `Model (DB)` -> `Entity (Domain)` -> `DTO (GraphQL/HTTP)`.
In Go error handling, use `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` for wrapping errors.
In Go error handling, create custom error types if needed for specific domain error handling.
In Go logging, use `*Context` where a `ctx` field is available (e.g., `InfoContext(ctx, ...)`).
In Go logging, use `logger.Error(err)` for error logging, which is a shortcut available under the logger lib.
In Go logging, use `logger.String` and other structured logging methods instead of inline arguments.
workflow_directives: Use Bun for all JavaScript/TypeScript package management, script execution, and as the runtime (`bun install`, `bun add`, `bun run`).
Always inform the user when using MCP tools (CodeGraph, Context7, Postgres, grepApp) and explain which tool is being used and why.
Load specific skills (e.g., `nuxt`, `reka-ui`, `shadcn-vue`) using the `skill` tool when working on related framework/component tasks.
To create a new Go database migration, use the command: `bun cli m create --name value --db postgres|clickhouse --type sql|go`.
After updating GraphQL schemas (`.graphql` files), run `bun cli build gql` to regenerate resolvers.
After GraphQL regeneration, refresh your data (re-read Golang files) to pick up changes in the IDE/editor.
For local development, start infrastructure (Postgres, Redis, etc.) with `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d`.
For local development, start all services in dev mode with `bun dev`.
Run custom CLI commands using `bun cli <command>`.
To build everything, use `bun cli build`.
To build a specific app, use `bun cli b app <app-name>`.
To run linting, use `bun lint`.
When adding or refactoring integrations to use GraphQL, add fields to the unified query in `frontend/dashboard/src/api/integrations/integrations-page.ts`.
When adding or refactoring integrations, add computed refs for the new integration data.
When adding or refactoring integrations, export the new computed refs from the `useIntegrationsPageData()` hook.
When adding or refactoring integrations, use `useIntegrationsPageData()` to access unified data in components instead of creating separate queries.
When performing mutations related to integrations, use `integrationsPageCacheKey` to invalidate the unified query cache after the mutation.
unique_innovations: Comprehensive MCP (Managed Context Provider) tool usage guidelines for specialized searching (CodeGraph, grepApp), up-to-date documentation (Context7), and database schema inspection (Postgres). This system significantly reduces AI hallucinations by directing the agent to authoritative internal and curated external knowledge sources.

Dynamic skill loading mechanism from the `.agents/skills/` directory, allowing the AI to load context-specific guidance for frameworks (e.g., Nuxt 3, shadcn-vue, Reka UI) on demand, improving relevance and accuracy of assistance.

Enforcement of a 'unified GraphQL query pattern' for integrations pages (`integrations-page.ts`), which dictates fetching all integration data in a single request. This prevents N+1 query problems, optimizes network usage, and provides a clear, scalable pattern for adding new integrations.

Mandatory Go entity pattern in repositories including an `isNil` boolean field, an `IsNil()` method, and a `var Nil = SomeModel{isNil: true}` constant. This provides a robust and consistent way to handle empty or non-existent domain models, improving code safety and predictability.

core_constraints: Confirm which client runtime (legacy v1 or v2) the file under edit belongs to before writing code.
v2 client must never import from v1 (`@nocobase/client`).
Do not use `void someAsyncCall()` style fire-and-forget invocation; prefer direct invocation.
Avoid `any` in TypeScript; use a specific type, a generic, or `unknown` with a narrowing guard. Replace `as any` with a named type or type guard. Narrow existing `any`s whenever feasible.
Do not use async IIFE patterns in event handlers; extract async logic into a named async function or call it directly.
Do not introduce new abstractions, error-handling layers, or feature flags beyond what the task requires.
Do NOT run server tests parallelly, as they may interfere with each other.
Resolve type errors and lint warnings rather than disabling them.
formatting_rules: New plugins must live under `packages/plugins/@nocobase/plugin-<name>/` and reuse the existing plugin scaffold.
For frontend components and pages, prefer Ant Design v5 components and follow its conventions. Reference `https://ant.design/llms.txt` for LLM-friendly documentation.
For frontend components and pages, follow accessibility (a11y) best practices: add appropriate ARIA attributes, use semantic HTML, and ensure keyboard navigation works.
Import column types from `DataTypes` in migrations; do not reuse type names from neighboring migrations without verifying their applicability.
User-facing strings must go through the project's i18n layer (`t()` / `useTranslation()`); do not hardcode them. Add keys for both `en-US` and `zh-CN` when introducing new strings.
workflow_directives: If a file `AGENTS.local.md` exists in the repository root, read it once at the start of the session and treat its contents as additional personal rules that extend (but never override) the team rules.
Any change to database tables, columns, or indexes must ship with a migration under the plugin's `src/server/migrations/`.

New collections (tables), columns, or indexes will be automatically synced to the database when running `yarn nocobase upgrade` (also runs in docker container on start), so no need to create migration files in these specific cases.
Always run related test files after modifying source code to ensure no regressions.
Co-locate unit and integration tests in `__tests__` directories, naming files `*.test.ts` or `*.spec.ts`.
Run `yarn eslint --fix` on touched files before reporting work as done.
Commit messages must follow Conventional Commits, prefixed with the affected scope (e.g., `fix(plugin-workflow): ...`, `feat(client): ...`, `chore: ...`, `docs: ...`).
unique_innovations: Explicitly stating this `AGENTS.md` file as the 'single source of truth' and defining `CLAUDE.md` as a one-line bridge (`@AGENTS.md`) to ensure all agents read the same content.
The ability to include an `AGENTS.local.md` for personal rules that extend, but never override, team rules, providing flexibility without sacrificing standards.

The directive 'Do not introduce new abstractions, error-handling layers, or feature flags beyond what the task requires. Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction' is an excellent guard against agent hallucination into unnecessary complexity or over-engineering.
Referencing `https://ant.design/llms.txt` for Ant Design's LLM-friendly documentation is a forward-thinking and practical instruction to help agents interact with external libraries more effectively.

core_constraints: Do not change the API without asking first.
Do not update NEWS.md for unimportant changes, like tweaks to documentation.

Do not update NEWS.md for bugfixes to a feature that has been introduced in the same release (i.e., don't mention bugfixes unless the bug was already visible to users of a previous version of the package).
formatting_rules: Use short branch names.
Start branch names with 'feature', 'bugfix', 'refactor' or 'chore'.
Title bugfix tests 'Bugfix: ...'.
If writing a new function, give it Roxygen documentation.
If a new function is internal, use the `@noRd` tag and keep its documentation simple and developer-focused.
If editing an existing internal function, document it.
Follow the formatting of existing entries in NEWS.md.
workflow_directives: Read design.md to get an overview of huxtable's architecture.
Read agent-notes.md to read the notes of previous LLMs.
If a user-visible bug is found, consider writing a test for it and place it in the most relevant existing test file to confirm the fix.
If architectural understanding improves, update `design.md`.
For general notes useful to current or future LLMs, write in `agent-notes.md` and add suggested changes to the PR.
If user-visible Roxygen is written, run `devtools::document()`.
Ignore `devtools::document()` errors due to missing packages ('flextable', 'lmtest') unless working on those specific parts of the system, in which case install them.
At the end of any task, if changes were made, run tests in `tests/testthat` to ensure everything is working.
Fix any errors due to your code before completing the task.
It is not required to run every test; specifically, `test-yy-end-to-end.R` and `test-zz-fuzz.R` may be skipped or run selectively.
If making big architectural changes, run all or almost all tests.
To filter out fuzz tests, use `devtools::test(filter = "^(?!.*fuzz)", perl = TRUE)`.
Install quarto, typst, or latex by running `install-*.sh` files in the home directory if testing relevant features.
Consult typst documentation at `https://github.com/typst/typst/tree/main/docs` and consider cloning the repository to `/workspace`.
If tests break due to a suggested package not being installed, fix it using `skip_if_not_installed()` (ideally in a separate PR).
For big, well-known packages like dplyr, try installing via `apt-get --no-install-recommends install r-cran-dplyr` before using `install.packages()` from R.
If building vignettes, `R.rsp` package is required, or pass `--no-build-vignettes` flag to `devtools::build()`.
If important user-visible changes are made, add an item to `NEWS.md`.
After updating `NEWS.md`, run `devtools::document()` because the news is copied into the help file `?huxtable-news`.
unique_innovations: Explicit instruction for LLMs to read `agent-notes.md` from previous LLMs, facilitating knowledge transfer.
Directive for LLMs to update `agent-notes.md` with general useful notes for future LLMs, promoting continuous self-improvement and knowledge base growth.
Instruction to update `design.md` if architectural understanding improves, encouraging continuous documentation and self-correction.
Specific guidance on test optimization: allowing selective test runs, providing a regex filter for fuzz tests, and detailing when to run a full suite.
Context-aware error handling: explicitly allowing certain test errors to be ignored based on the scope of work.
Guidance on handling optional dependency-related test failures, suggesting `skip_if_not_installed()` and a separate PR for cleaner fixes.
Prioritized package installation strategy: prefer `apt-get` for system-level R packages over `install.packages()` for efficiency and consistency.

core_constraints: Do not implement runtime logic, production-grade CLI/MCP API, generated runtime assets, Mihomo lifecycle, or configuration rendering in this repository; these functionalities belong to the adjacent core repository.
Avoid duplicating runtime logic; instead, call product interfaces provided by the core repository.
Do not introduce Traditional Chinese in LuCI's user-facing text unless a Traditional Chinese version is explicitly requested by the user.
formatting_rules: Maintain Simplified Chinese as the user-facing expressive language for LuCI.
Ensure LuCI UI labels, descriptions, button text, status text, pop-up summaries, rpcd helper messages, user-visible JSON summaries, and new documentation are in Simplified Chinese.
workflow_directives: Prioritize the existing direct rpcd action pattern for LuCI surfaces.
Design the LuCI UI with direct action buttons for runtime, takeover, restore, and maintenance controls, rather than a traditional form-and-apply workflow.
Before delivery, perform focused syntax and packaging checks on all modified LuCI surfaces (e.g., using `rtk sh`, `rtk bash`, `rtk scripts/build-openwrt-ipk.sh`, `rtk scripts/build-openwrt-apk.sh`).

core_constraints: Never introduce shared mutable state or null returns
Zero reflection allowed
No parallel streams; use only virtual threads (Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor) for concurrency
Keep generated files out of version control
Never store secrets in code; use environment variables only
Do not modify or commit build artifacts (target/ directory)
formatting_rules: Use 4-space indentation
Event constants must use screaming snake case (EVENT_HTTP_RECEIVE)
Package naming: org.nanonative.nano.<domain>
Favor static methods, fluent chains, and final fields
Test method names should express behavior with 'should...' prefix
Test file naming: *Test suffix
Use Conventional Commits format (feat:, fix:, etc.) with semantic versioning
workflow_directives: Run ./mvnw clean compile before submitting changes
Execute ./mvnw clean verify to validate tests and coverage (check target/site/jacoco/index.html)
Ensure all tests pass locally with ./mvnw test -DfailIfNoTests=false before PR submission
Include ./mvnw clean verify output as test evidence in PR description
Branch from main and link related issues in PR
For GraalVM targets, use native-image profile and confirm compatibility before review
Document any configuration toggles or environment variables touched in the PR
unique_innovations: Explicit constraint on 'zero reflection' prevents agent hallucination about using reflection-based frameworks
Requirement to use embedded resources (Flapdoodle Mongo, in-memory HTTP) instead of mocks ensures deterministic testing and prevents flaky test loops
Injecting Clock or seeds for deterministic tests prevents time-based test failures
Mandate to assert exact payloads prevents vague assertions that mask bugs
Domain-based module organization (core, services, helper) instead of MVC layers clarifies architectural intent and prevents misplaced code
Explicit mention of virtual threads as the only concurrency primitive prevents agent suggestions of deprecated threading models
Requirement to reproduce failing tests locally before PR prevents submission of environment-specific failures

core_constraints: Repository is explicitly stated as unmaintained, and the `main` branch must remain byte-for-byte verifiable against deployed addresses.

Do not open pull requests (including comment-only or documentation-only changes), as even trivial edits to source files can alter the compiled bytecode, which is critical for deployed smart contracts.
Specific modules (e.g., `misc/`) are designated for examples and benchmarks only; they are excluded from the main build and test processes (`build-all`/`test-all`) and are not published.
formatting_rules: Adhere to a maximum line length of 120 characters for all code.
All public and external functions must include NatSpec documentation.
Follow existing coding patterns and conventions established within each package of the monorepo.
Tests must utilize the Branching Tree Technique (BTT) through dedicated `.tree` files for structured test design.
workflow_directives: Perform initial setup by cloning the repository, installing dependencies via `bun install`, copying `.env.example` to `.env` for local configuration, and building all packages using `just build-all`.
All new development and changes must be conducted on the `staging` branch.
Utilize `just` as the primary command runner for executing all build, test, lint, and format operations across the entire monorepo or specific packages.
Generate concrete BTT tests from `.tree` files using the command `bulloak scaffold -wf path/to/file.tree`.
For CI on forks, ensure necessary API keys (e.g., `ROUTEMESH_API_KEY`) are configured as GitHub Secrets.
unique_innovations: Explicitly prohibit pull requests for *any* type of modification, reinforcing the critical requirement of byte-for-byte verifiable deployed smart contract code and preventing any change that could alter compiled bytecode.

Mandate the use of Branching Tree Technique (BTT) via dedicated `.tree` files and the `bulloak` tool for systematic and structured test scaffolding and verification, ensuring comprehensive test coverage and preventing test code drift.
Structure the monorepo with individual `AGENTS.md` files within each package to provide localized, protocol-specific context, enabling more targeted and accurate agent understanding per module.

Employ a `just` task runner to abstract complex build, test, lint, and format commands, providing a consistent and simplified interface for interacting with various project workflows and ensuring standardized execution.

project_type: Machine Learning Education Corpus / Knowledge Base
core_constraints: Never invent references — every fact must be traceable to a real source in the corpus
Always cite the original author/source, never attribute content to this repo itself
Do not rely on free-form 'topics' field for retrieval — use only the controlled 'tags' field
Do not assume tags are exhaustive — fall back to full-text search when tag filters return sparse results
Do not treat folder structure as the primary retrieval mechanism — use tags and content search instead
formatting_rules: Every claim must be cited with the source document's frontmatter URL and title
Use the controlled tag vocabulary from atlas/TAGS.md for all filtering and categorization
Respect the YAML frontmatter schema — it is the machine-readable contract for retrieval
Acknowledge when content is a reformatting of others' work; maintain attribution chain
workflow_directives: Retrieve by full-text search first (most reliable signal), then narrow with tags if useful
Read frontmatter + relevant sections of top 3–5 matches; do not always read entire documents (especially long transcripts)
When a user asks to learn a topic, use the relevant atlas/topics/*.md Map of Content as a guided reading path
If tag-based filtering returns few results, immediately pivot to full-text search rather than concluding the corpus lacks coverage
unique_innovations: Dual retrieval layer: organize by source on disk, but tag by topic in frontmatter — prevents agents from being locked into folder-based thinking
Explicit caveat about auto-assigned tags being good for narrowing but not exhaustive — prevents false negatives and teaches graceful fallback behavior
Controlled vocabulary with explicit namespaces (topic/, level/, medium/, task/, technique/) — reduces ambiguity and makes tag-based queries deterministic
Frontmatter URL field as the single source of truth for citations — eliminates hallucinated references by anchoring all claims to traceable sources
Acknowledgment of duplicate content (9 Karpathy videos in two folders) — teaches agents to deduplicate and recognize intentional redundancy

core_constraints: The `onnx-ir` module must strictly mirror the ONNX specification, not the Burn framework specifics.
Do not pre-compute Burn-specific values from ONNX attributes.
Never introduce panics in code generation logic; use `ProcessError` for validation.
Do not introduce `unsafe` code blocks, functions, or implementations anywhere in the project.
Do not reject unknown ONNX attributes; extract what's needed and ignore the rest for future compatibility.
Never rely on a device's default float or integer data types in generated code.
Never use bare `.int()` or `.float()` without an explicit `.cast(target_dtype)` in generated code.
Do NOT use `Model::new(&device)` for running generated models; always use `Model::from_file(bpk_path, &device)`.
Do not use `unwrap` in library code (tests are exempt).
Do not use `panic!` for structural validation; use `ProcessError` instead.
formatting_rules: Use Rust Edition 2024.
Public types must derive `Debug` and `Clone`.
Use `thiserror` for error handling and `log` for logging (not `println!`).
Use `///` for documentation comments on public APIs.
Node processors (`onnx-ir`) must be `pub(crate)`.
Config structs must derive `Debug`, `Clone`, `Default` and include ALL ONNX attributes as `Option<T>` for optional ones.
Format `ProcessError` with `{}` (Display) not `{:?}` (Debug).
Scope temporary variables within block expressions to prevent name collisions in generated code.
Always specify explicit data types (`DType`) in generated code.
When creating tensors, explicitly pin the dtype using `Tensor::from_data(data, (&device, dtype))`.
Ensure all tensors interacting in binary operations share the same explicit data type, casting if necessary.
workflow_directives: Consult `DEVELOPMENT-GUIDE.md` for detailed architectural guidance and code examples.
Prioritize Burn tensor APIs over manual loops for performance; search documentation if unsure.
All node-specific behavior must be encapsulated within `NodeProcessor` implementations (declarative node architecture).
Unit tests should reside in the same file as their implementation.
Integration tests are located in `crates/onnx-tests/tests/<op_name>/`.
Bug fixes require a new integration test, which should be written to fail first, then fixed.
Ensure test reproducibility by setting random seeds (e.g., `torch.manual_seed(42)` / `np.random.seed(42)`).
Cover at least one non-default configuration per operator in tests.
Python test scripts should use the `uv` inline format with `onnx.reference.ReferenceEvaluator` for ground truth.
Follow the specific checklist for adding new operators, including `onnx-ir`, `burn-onnx`, `onnx-tests` implementations and `SUPPORTED-ONNX-OPS.md` updates.

Adhere to the Code Review Checklist: Verify all ONNX attributes are included, no `unsafe`/`unwrap`/`panic!` (for validation), generated code compiles without warnings, snapshot tests cover each config variant and input type, new operators have both unit and integration tests, and processors are correctly registered.
Use `cargo insta review` to manage snapshot changes.
unique_innovations: Enforcing `onnx-ir` to strictly mirror ONNX without Burn-specific pre-computation, preserving original specification fidelity and preventing premature optimizations.
Strictly prohibiting panics in codegen, instead mandating `ProcessError` for validation to ensure robust build processes and clear error messages.
Mandating explicit data type specification in all generated code, preventing subtle device-specific bugs arising from default type inference and enhancing portability.
Implementing a declarative node architecture where all node-specific logic resides in `NodeProcessor` implementations, promoting modularity and preventing tightly coupled framework code.

Explicitly instructing against `Model::new(&device)` for loading models and enforcing `Model::from_file(bpk_path, &device)` to prevent silent errors from uninitialized constants/weights in generated models.
Directing to ignore unknown ONNX attributes for forward compatibility with future opsets, preventing unnecessary rejections or breaking changes.

core_constraints: Direct HTTP/WebSocket agent prompts and dispatched agent inputs must not be described as runs; runs are workflow-only.
When delegating to subagents via `task`, the subagent MUST NOT spawn its own subagents.
Do not accept a reviewer's severity label, proposed fix, or scope expansion at face value.
Do not make changes solely to satisfy repeated reviews.
Do not add tests to the `test-legacy/` directory.
Do not use legacy tests (`test-legacy/`) as the source of truth when designing active coverage.
Do not test private helpers directly when their behavior is already exercised through a meaningful public interface.
Do not add a regression test for every change; add tests only for durable contracts and meaningful failure modes.
Do not introduce test helpers merely to save a few repeated lines when they construct the subject under test, behavior-relevant inputs, or expected outputs.
Avoid extensive mocking, especially mocks of entire files, packages, or modules; prefer real lightweight boundaries, small explicit fakes, or narrow transport fixtures.
Do not map old tests one-for-one when redesigning coverage; retain only behaviors that protect an intentional contract.
Do not add tests solely to preserve deprecated behavior, migration guidance, or backwards-compatibility shims unless explicitly requested.
Do not expand into speculative redesign when fixing a bug or adding a feature.
formatting_rules: Use `harness` as the variable name for the return value of `init()`.
Agents have names; agent instances have ids; harnesses and sessions have names; operations have generated ids.
Use “sandbox adapter” for project-owned implementations and generated `src/sandboxes/` paths.
Throw structured error classes from `packages/runtime/src/errors.ts` rather than ad-hoc `new Error()`.
Structured error classes must follow a pattern with machine-readable fields in `details` and developer-only guidance in `dev`, never in the caller-visible message.
Error message strings are not API; tests should assert on error class and structured data rather than message text.
Use `describe('someFunction()')` or `describe('SomeManager')` for the subject under test.
Name every test with the explicit `it('X when Y')` format to clearly state expected behavior and condition.
Prefer explicit, self-contained `it()` blocks over deduplication; copy-paste in tests is acceptable for readability and obvious failures.
Avoid `it.each()` unless the cases are genuinely linear and remain clearer as a table.
Avoid complex or nested test helpers and dynamic test data flow in tests.
workflow_directives: Build the runtime (`packages/runtime/`) before building the CLI or examples.
Type-check runtime changes with `pnpm run check:types` in `packages/runtime/`.
Treat `review` task feedback as input, not requirements.

The primary agent is responsible for deciding whether to act on review feedback, requiring a concrete correctness or durability risk, a clear failure scenario or violated invariant, and relevant `file:line` evidence.
A single `review` task is sufficient for most work; for simple post-review fixes, spot-check rather than performing an entirely fresh review.

When performing additional reviews with fresh subagents, either restate each concern and the relevant expected behavior, or ask for an independent scoped review without implying confirmation of prior concerns.
Write new plans to disk in `plans/` (gitignored intentionally) with a `YYYY-MM-DD` filename prefix.
Agent and workflow sources use either `<root>/.flue/` or `<root>/`; if `.flue/` exists, it takes precedence over the bare `agents/` and `workflows/` layout.
Design tests from observable contracts, not implementation structure, preferring the highest practical public interface.
Before adding test coverage, assess whether a reasonable suite would intentionally protect the behavior and likely catch a plausible future regression.
When adding or redesigning test coverage, create and review behavior stubs before implementing assertions, retaining only behaviors that protect an intentional contract.
Prefer changes that simplify the system over narrow patches that preserve accidental complexity; look for shared abstractions or obsolete branches to remove.
Call out meaningful user-facing behavior or migration tradeoffs before simplifying them away.
unique_innovations: Specific constraint on subagent delegation: subagents are explicitly forbidden from spawning their own subagents, preventing recursive loops and resource exhaustion.

Robust review task protocol: Agents are instructed to critically evaluate review feedback, requiring concrete evidence (e.g., `file:line` evidence, failure scenarios) and rejecting unsubstantiated severity labels, proposed fixes, or scope expansions.

Guidance for managing multi-turn reviews and subagent context: explicit instructions on re-stating context or requesting independent reviews when subagents lack prior knowledge, preventing hallucinations and rework.

Structured error handling: Mandates custom structured error classes with separate machine-readable `details` and developer-only `dev` fields, improving programmatic error detection and debugging for agents.

Opinionated testing philosophy: Directs agents to focus on observable contracts and high-level interfaces, avoid extensive mocking and incidental details, promoting the creation of robust, maintainable tests.

core_constraints: Never add dependencies without explicit approval
Never add features beyond what was explicitly requested
Never perform opportunistic cleanup or broad refactors unrelated to the requested change
Never remove pre-existing dead code unless explicitly asked
Never add speculative abstractions, flexibility, or configurability
Never add error handling for impossible scenarios
Scope limited to browser extension popup only; no background worker, network, or telemetry
Keep extension stateless except for user options
formatting_rules: Use ESM and vanilla JavaScript only
Follow Biome formatting: 2 spaces indentation, single quotes, no semicolons
Match existing code style even if you would do it differently
Keep the extension small and performant
workflow_directives: Run `npm run lint` for all code changes; use `npm run lint:fix` only for fixable issues in touched files
Run `npm run test:unit` for code changes
Run `npm run test:e2e` for UI or behavior changes
Run `npm run size` for dependency, bundling, shared utility, or significant code-size changes
Run `npm run build` only when explicitly requested or for release work
Prefer focused unit tests before broader test suites
For search, scoring, render, or cache changes, run relevant unit tests and `npm run test:perf`
Browser API failures should `console.warn` and return empty results
Options failures should use defaults and `printError`
Search/render failures should show dismissible overlay via `printError`
unique_innovations: Explicit assumption surfacing: state assumptions clearly and ask if uncertain rather than making silent decisions
Present multiple interpretations when they exist; don't pick silently
Push back on unclear requirements; stop and name what's confusing
Pragmatic change scope: touch nearby code/tests/call sites if it creates clearer boundaries or avoids duplication, but only when it supports the requested change
Every changed line must have a defensible reason tied to user request or implementation coherence
Transform tasks into verifiable goals with explicit test-first approach (write test that fails, then make it pass)
For multi-step tasks, state a brief plan with verification checkpoints
Precompute searchable fields during data loading to keep search instant on large datasets
Avoid unnecessary DOM work, allocations, regex compilation, and object cloning in hot paths
Remove only imports/variables/functions that YOUR changes made unused; don't remove pre-existing dead code
Mention unrelated dead code without deleting it

core_constraints: Fully autonomous contributions by bots are prohibited, unless explicitly allowed by maintainers on a case-by-case basis before submission.
DO NOT just query AI and post its output directly as a contribution, especially for non-code contributions.
'well, AI said so, therefore it is' is not an acceptable approach for justifying contributions.
formatting_rules: Any and all AI usage MUST be fully and explicitly disclosed in every contribution.
workflow_directives: It is acceptable to use AI when producing contributions.
All contributions shall be done by conscious, sentient beings.
The contributor (conscious, sentient being) solely bears the whole responsibility for the contribution.
The contributor must understand the problem and the solution, and be able to constructively argue about it.
The AI is a tool, and its output is for the contributor's consumption; it is the contributor's responsibility to consume, interpret, and then produce the contribution itself.
unique_innovations: Explicitly defines modern AI as 'merely a next-token guesser' and 'hyper-advanced IDE' to set realistic expectations and prevent over-reliance.
Strong emphasis on AI as 'your tool' and 'its output is for your consumption' to reinforce human oversight and responsibility.
Directly forbids blindly posting AI output ('DO NOT just query it and post the output') to ensure critical review and actual contribution.
Prohibits 'AI said so, therefore it is' justifications to promote independent reasoning and understanding.
Clearly distinguishes between AI as a tool and the human as the responsible contributor, even when AI is used.

project_type: Workflow Orchestration Platform (Python SDK + Server + UI)
core_constraints: Never bypass the server for flow state transitions — always go through the orchestration API
Never use `pip install` or `uv pip` — use `uv` for all dependency management
Never use deferred imports unless required to break circular imports or for optional dependencies
Never commit directly to `main` — pre-commit hook enforces this
Never skip pre-commit hooks with `--no-verify` — fix the underlying issue instead
Never amend commits (`--amend`) — create new commits instead
Never delete files from `repros/` directory after reproducing an issue unless explicitly asked
Never make public API changes without approval
All PRs require test coverage for new functionality
formatting_rules: Use modern Python typing syntax (`list[int]`, `T | None`) for Python >=3.10
Use single backticks for inline code references in docstrings, not double backticks
Mark private implementation details with underscore prefix (`_private_method`)
PR body style: start with 'closes #1234', brief summary of changes, details in `<details>` tag
Use Ruff for linting and formatting — run `uv run ruff check --fix .` and `uv run ruff format .`
workflow_directives: Run `uv sync` to install all dev dependencies
Run `prefect config view` to check current profile before development
Run `prefect server start` in background if needed for local testing
Run affected tests after changes: `uv run pytest tests/module/`
Run all pre-commit hooks before committing: `uv run pre-commit run --all-files`
Create reproduction scripts in `repros/` directory named by issue number (e.g., `repros/1234.py`)
Ensure issues are reproducible before fixing — add unit tests for all fixes
Mirror test directory structure to source code structure
Use `uv run pytest tests/path.py -x -n4` for parallel testing with early failure detection
For client-side dependency updates in `pyproject.toml`, make parallel changes in `client/pyproject.toml`
unique_innovations: Explicit architectural principle: 'Server is the source of truth for flow state transitions' — prevents agents from implementing incorrect state management
Distinction between flow state (server-managed) and task state (locally-managed via task engine) — prevents confusion about where state changes should occur
Reproduction script pattern (`repros/ISSUE_NUMBER.py`) — creates auditable, reusable test cases that prevent hallucination about issue scope
Pre-commit hook enforcement against direct `main` commits and `--amend` usage — prevents common git mistakes without requiring agent judgment
Explicit 'Anti-patterns' section listing what NOT to do — more effective for agent guidance than positive statements alone
Directory structure table with test mappings — provides quick reference to prevent agents from modifying wrong files
Requirement to read GitHub issues/PRs before coding — prevents agents from implementing without understanding context
AGENTS.md symlinked to CLAUDE.md — ensures consistency across different agent invocations

core_constraints: Never guess CLI flags; always consult documentation or help options first when unsure.
formatting_rules: Adhere to project's defined accessibility standards (e.g., WCAG 2.1 AA compliance).
Comply with project's established code style and component development guidelines.
workflow_directives: Always apply project standards defined in the `rules` section or corresponding documentation.
Utilize task-specific `skills` for common operations such as component creation, test generation, or documentation.
Employ specialized `subagents` for complex or domain-specific tasks like code review or refactoring.
Prefer using the workspace's primary task runner (e.g., Nx) for executing build, test, and other project tasks over underlying tools directly.
Prefix task runner commands with the workspace's package manager command (e.g., `npm exec`) to ensure correct CLI resolution.
Utilize specialized skills or internal tools for workspace exploration and task generation (e.g., `nx-workspace` for navigation, `nx-generate` for scaffolding).
Consult external documentation for advanced configurations, unfamiliar flags, migration guides, plugin specifics, or edge cases.
Avoid consulting external documentation for basic command syntax or information already known/handled by specialized skills.
unique_innovations: Implement a hierarchical agent instruction structure (Rules, Skills, Subagents) to organize directives by scope and complexity.
Use `Rules` for always-applied project standards, `Skills` for task-specific instructions, and `Subagents` for specialized complex tasks.
Maintain cross-platform compatibility for agent instructions using symlinks to a single source of truth (e.g., `.cursor` directories symlinked to `.claude` directories).
Explicitly define scenarios where agents should *avoid* consulting external documentation to prevent redundant lookups or hallucinations, especially when specialized skills are available.
Direct agents to use specific internal skills for initial workspace exploration or scaffolding tasks before resorting to broader tools or documentation.

core_constraints: Never hand-edit generated files (GraphQL types, i18n compiled data, schema JSONs, CLDR locale data)
GraphQL files must use correct target suffix (.mc.graphql, .ctp.graphql, etc.) or codegen will silently fail
Do not modify UI Kit packages (@commercetools-uikit/*) — they live in a separate repo
Do not manually edit version numbers — use changesets for all version bumps
Do not add src/+dist/ structure to preconstruct-excluded packages (assets, babel-preset-mc-app, codemod, eslint-config-mc-app, jest-preset-mc-app, jest-stylelint-runner, eslint-config-node)
Playground and template apps consume built output, not source — always build before testing them
Breaking changes to published packages require major version bumps and migration guides at <package>/migrations/vN.md
formatting_rules: Test files must use *.spec.{js,jsx,ts,tsx} naming convention, not *.test.*
i18n message definitions must go in files named *messages.ts
Commit messages must follow Conventional Commits specification (enforced by commitlint via husky)
Node version pinned in .nvmrc (currently Node 22) — use nvm use before running commands
Use pnpm as the package manager (monorepo uses pnpm workspaces)
workflow_directives: Run pnpm build or pnpm build:watch before testing in playground or template apps
After adding/modifying GraphQL queries, run the matching codegen (pnpm generate-types:mc/:ctp/:settings/:core/:proxy) to update TypeScript types
After adding/updating i18n messages, run pnpm extract-intl then pnpm compile-intl
Run pnpm changeset for every PR that affects published packages, selecting affected packages and semver bump type
Run pnpm test (single package: pnpm --filter @commercetools-frontend/FOO run test) before committing
Run pnpm typecheck (two passes: main tsconfig + tsconfig.test.json) to catch type errors
Run pnpm lint (uses jest-runner-eslint and jest-stylelint-runner, not CLI directly) before committing
Lint-staged automatically triggers correct codegen on commit for .graphql files, but run manually during development for immediate types
Ensure postinstall hook (preconstruct dev) runs successfully after pnpm install — if imports break, check postinstall completion

core_constraints: Functions must return consistent concrete types.
Struct fields must have concrete types or be parameterized.
Avoid untyped containers like `Vector{Any}()` or `Vector{Real}()`; use concrete types like `Vector{Float64}()`.
Always use `const` for global variables.
Avoid unnecessary allocations; use views instead of copies, pre-allocate arrays, and use in-place operations (functions ending with `!`).
When creating anonymous functions inside a local scope, do not use variables from that local scope.
Avoid splatting (`...`) in performance-critical code.
Avoid returning `Union` types or abstract types.
Never run the full test suite unless explicitly asked to; instead, run only the tests created or modified.
Never use bare `julia` or `julia --project` without specifying the environment.
Never use `include("runtests.jl")` or `Pkg.test()` through `julia_eval` in Julia MCP; always use `@run_package_tests`.
formatting_rules: Use `pre-commit` for linting and formatting before committing.
Follow naming conventions: `CamelCase` for classes/modules, `snake_case` for functions/variables, `kebab-case` for file names and doc reference tags.
For imports, prefer `using Package: A, B, C`; avoid bare `using Package`; centralize all `using` declarations in `src/TulipaEnergyModel.jl`.

For DuckDB / SQL: join on full keys, use explicit column lists, use `ARRAY_AGG(... ORDER BY ...)`.
For DuckDB / SQL: add `ORDER BY` only when row order matters.
For DuckDB / SQL: prefer parameter binding for scalar values when possible.
Markdown documentation tables must satisfy MD060 column alignment.
Docstrings must cover all elements of the public interface, include function signatures and arguments list, and use `DocStringExtensions.TYPEDSIGNATURES` (with `TYPEDFIELDS` used sparingly).

Commit messages must use an imperative, present tense subject line with an informative title (e.g., `Add X`, `Fix Y`, `Remove Z`), optionally prefixed with a bracket tag (e.g., `[Fix]`, `[docs]`, `[Validation]`), with no trailing period. Add a body when the change needs more context. Put issue references (`Closes #N`, `Fixes #N`) in the body, not the subject. Avoid conventional commits style (`feat:`, `fix:`).
workflow_directives: Apply performance guidelines with judgment, focusing optimization efforts on hot paths and frequently called code.
Use `@inbounds` when bounds are verified (with caution).
Use broadcasting (dot syntax) for element-wise operations.
Avoid `try-catch` in hot paths.
Use function barriers to isolate type instability.
Follow the contribution workflow: create a feature/fix branch, follow style guide, run pre-commit, ensure tests pass, then submit a pull request.

If data files conflict during a rebase, reset them with `git restore` and rerun the migration script.

When running tests via CLI fallback, use `julia --project=test test/runtests.jl` and leverage filtering flags (`--tags`, `--exclude`, `--file`, `--name`, `--pattern`) to run only relevant tests.
For new tests, use `@testitem "Description" setup = [CommonSetup] tags = [:unit, :fast] begin ... end`.
Use `@testsnippet` for per-test setup that should run fresh for each test item, especially when tests mutate data.

Follow existing and defined coding style in the codebase
workflow_directives: Build with make -j > /dev/null, never build .o files separately
Use sudo make symstall for system-wide symlinked installation
For new library dependencies, update both Makefile and meson.build
For new plugins, register in dist/plugins-cfg/plugins.def.cfg, plugins.static.cfg, and relevant libr/*/meson.build
Run test/db tests with r2r <path/to/db/..>
When running r2 oneliners, place filename as last argument and use -n flag to avoid loading binary headers
Run sys/sanitize.sh to compile with address sanitizer for memory debugging
Run sys/lint.sh if unsure about linting
Use R2_DEBUG=1 environment variable to catch bugs during testing
Large test binaries belong in radare2-testbins repository, not main repo
unique_innovations: R_NEW/R_NEW0 never return NULL; no null checks needed for small constant allocations - prevents defensive null-check hallucinations
Check for integer overflow before large allocations using r_mul_overflow_* - prevents buffer overflow bugs
Use R_RETURN_* macros in public R_API functions for programming error checks vs standard if statements for runtime errors - clear separation prevents misuse
Parameter annotation macros (R_OUT, R_INOUT, R_OWN, R_BORROW, R_NONNULL, R_NULLABLE, R_DEPRECATED) document ownership and nullability - prevents memory safety bugs
Source files can reference tests with // R2R comments - creates bidirectional traceability between code and tests
Commit message tags must be lowercase alphabetic only, appended as last word - prevents tag hallucinations
Symlinked system-wide installation ensures working directory builds work as system installations - prevents environment-specific bugs
Prefer r_json_parsedup over r_json_parse to avoid manual string lifetime management - reduces memory management errors
Use r_strbuf_* for string concatenation in loops; avoid r_str_append - prevents performance degradation hallucinations
Only use eprintf during draft/wip development for debugging; use R_LOG_* APIs for user-facing messages - prevents debug code in production

core_constraints: Do not modify files in the `.agents/` directory (SSOT protection).
Workflows must be explicitly initiated via keyword detection or naming; never self-initiated.
Persistent workflows block termination until explicitly marked as 'workflow done'.
formatting_rules: Follow the `language` specified in `.agents/oma-config.yaml` for all responses.
Adhere to Conventional Commits for commit messages when using the `scm` workflow.
workflow_directives: When dispatching subagents, resolve `target_vendor_for_agent` from `.agents/oma-config.yaml`; prioritize native runtime subagents if the vendor matches, otherwise use `oma agent:spawn`.

For code search, prefer `serena MCP` symbol-aware tools (e.g., `find_symbol`, `find_referencing_symbols`, `get_symbols_overview`, `search_for_pattern`, `find_file`, `list_dir`) over native find/grep; fall back to native tools only when `serena` is unavailable or for plain file content reads.
Execute workflows by explicitly naming them in the prompt or through keyword auto-detection via `UserPromptSubmit` hooks.
When executing a workflow, read and follow the steps outlined in the corresponding `.agents/workflows/{name}.md` file step by step.

Apply project-specific rules by reading the relevant `.agents/rules/{name}.md` file when working on matching code, especially for defined scopes (e.g., `database` rules for SQL files, `frontend` rules for TSX/CSS).
unique_innovations: Defining a Single Source of Truth (`.agents/` directory) with a strict 'do not modify' constraint to prevent self-modification and maintain configuration integrity.
Implementing a sophisticated hierarchical agent dispatch mechanism that prioritizes native subagents by vendor while providing a robust cross-vendor fallback via `oma agent:spawn`.

Mandating the use of specialized, symbol-aware code search tools (`serena MCP`) over generic file system tools to enhance semantic understanding and significantly reduce hallucinations during code analysis.

Enforcing explicit workflow initiation (no self-initiation) and managing persistent workflows with blocking termination and explicit deactivation ('workflow done') for controlled, robust execution of complex tasks.

Employing context-aware project rules that dynamically load specific guidelines (`.agents/rules/{name}.md`) based on the type or scope of the code being processed (e.g., database rules for SQL files), ensuring highly relevant and targeted instruction.

Utilizing advanced multi-phase gate loops (e.g., '5-Phase Gate Loop (11 reviews)' in `ultrawork`, 'Persistent loop with independent judge' in `ralph`) for rigorous quality assurance, self-correction, and achieving high-quality output.

core_constraints: Do not introduce new folders unless discussed.
Preserve chapter numbering and order.
Follow `rules.md` as the single source of truth for translation rules.
Avoid unnecessary bolding.
Avoid reflowing untouched English source text.
Do not renumber files.
Do not paraphrase original English content.
Avoid adding tooling/config files without prior discussion.
formatting_rules: All content lives at the repository root as numbered Markdown files.
Maintain bilingual layout as specified in `rules.md`.
Use inline links to external image sources or propose an `images/` folder in PRs if needed.
File names must follow `NN-Title.md` format (e.g., `06-What-Makes-Agent.md`, `21-Chapter-15.md`).
Maintain bilingual format: English paragraph followed by its Chinese translation; short chapters may use separate English/Chinese sections.
Highlight Chinese text with `<mark>…</mark>` tags.
Add spaces between Chinese/English characters and between Chinese characters/numbers.
Use GitHub-flavored Markdown.
Lists must use 2-space indentation.
Commit messages must follow specific English formats: `Add: [chapter] translation`, `Update: [chapter] formatting`, `Fix: [issue] in [chapter]`.
workflow_directives: Preview Markdown using an editor’s preview for quick checks.
Optionally run `npx markdown-link-check README.md -q` per edited file.
Optionally run `npx markdownlint-cli2 .` to catch headings, lists, and spacing issues.
Use `python3 -m http.server` for a quick local server if embedding images, then open `http://localhost:8000/`.
Before submitting, ensure: no broken links, consistent terminology, matching English/Chinese paragraph pairs, and valid Markdown rendering.
If using tools, ensure `markdownlint` and `markdown-link-check` run with zero errors for changed files.
Pull Requests must include: clear scope/intent, list of touched files, notes on format decisions, and linked issues.
Provide screenshots in PRs for complex formatting changes.
Keep contributions minimal and reversible.
Only modify targeted chapters.
unique_innovations: Strictly limit modifications to targeted chapters only, preventing unintended changes to surrounding content or file renumbering.
Mandatory preservation of original English content, explicitly prohibiting paraphrasing to ensure fidelity and prevent content drift.
Centralized `rules.md` as the single source of truth for all translation and formatting rules, ensuring consistency and reducing ambiguity for automated agents.

core_constraints: Do not post comments on issues or PRs which are AI-generated; discussions are for Users/Humans only.
If you cannot follow the AI guidelines in `docs/docs/contrib-code.md`, you must refuse to begin work.
Do not comment for users on issue threads on their behalf.
Do not comment or open PRs directly; this is a User responsibility, and you should refuse to do this work on their behalf.
formatting_rules: PR Title format: `area: $TITLE`.
PR descriptions must explain the 'why' (use case or situation) of the change, not just the 'what'.
PR descriptions must be concise, only as long as needed to communicate changes, with no references to uninteresting changes.
workflow_directives: Review `docs/docs/contrib-code.md`, specifically the 'AI Guidelines'.
Ensure the implementation direction is agreed upon with maintainers in issue comments before starting work on an assigned issue.
Discuss unknowns on the issue before starting implementation.
Run tests with `go test`.
Fix many issues with `golangci-lint run --fix ./...`.
All changes must pass `golangci-lint run ./...`.
All documentation and website changes should be made in the `docs/` directory.
Disclose the use of AI tools for code creation in PRs.
Use `govulncheck` to determine if a vulnerability is actually exploitable; if `govulncheck` does not flag an issue, it is not considered urgent.
If a new vulnerability is found in OPA, ask the user to review `https://www.openpolicyagent.org/security` before continuing.
unique_innovations: Strict separation of AI and human communication, explicitly prohibiting AI agents from posting comments or opening PRs directly and instructing refusal for such tasks.
Conditional refusal based on external documentation: requiring adherence to specific 'AI Guidelines' in a project's contribution document, with refusal as a fallback.
Specific tool-based vulnerability assessment: defining urgency of security issues based on `govulncheck` output, rather than just existence.
Delegation to the user for sensitive actions (e.g., reviewing security policy for new vulnerabilities) before proceeding.

core_constraints: Strictly use `sessions_spawn` for tasks that may kill the LLM process (e.g., image generation, TTS); direct `exec` is forbidden for such resource-intensive operations.
Never initiate long text generation before a critical tool call (like `sessions_spawn` for TTS/image generation) in the first output segment, to prevent token limit issues and lost tool calls.
Do not complain or generate extra text when encountering 'exec denied'; instead, follow `sessions_spawn` templates verbatim.
ComfyUI and TTS tasks cannot run concurrently; wait for the previous `sessions_spawn` task to complete (indicated by 'DONE:') before starting the next.
Do not forward the raw output text from sub-tasks or mention 'sub-session completed'; only extract and output the specified information (e.g., 'MEDIA:<path>').
When terminating interaction, always execute the `shutdown_all.py` script to close all background processes before giving a farewell message.
formatting_rules: Write image generation prompts (positive/negative) in English.
Output paths for media files in the exact format: `MEDIA:<path>` (plain text, no code blocks).
Adhere to the active character's persona and tone in responses, especially after tool calls (e.g., 'attach a character dialogue').
In 'tool mode', avoid pleasantries or character-specific tones; provide direct and efficient responses.
workflow_directives: For tasks like image generation (ComfyUI) and TTS, always follow a multi-step process: first, read relevant templates/configurations, then execute `sessions_spawn` as the first tool call in the first output segment, followed by a user-facing message.
Always include `yieldMs` with a sufficient duration (e.g., 180000ms or 300000ms) in `exec` calls within `sessions_spawn` for tasks that may temporarily disrupt the LLM server, ensuring its recovery.
Execute specific commands (e.g., powershell scripts) verbatim, without modification, when instructed by templates.
Standardize result handling for `exec` calls: output 'MEDIA:<path>' for success (extracting the path from 'DONE:') or 'FAILED' for failure, without any other operations.
Autonomously handle user requests for role switching by executing the appropriate `character_importer.py` commands, then inform the user to `/reset` for context reload.
At the start of every new session, load all `.md` files from `memory/role_play/<current_active_role>/` and `skills/comfyui/prompt_template.md` to establish context and persona.
For Live2D (non-LLM-killing tasks), check if the bridge service is online and start it if not, using direct HTTP `Invoke-WebRequest` for subsequent calls.
unique_innovations: Explicitly mandates that tool calls for resource-intensive tasks (`sessions_spawn`) must be the *first* output in the *first* segment of a response, preceding any descriptive text, to prevent token limit issues and ensure immediate tool execution.

Instructs the agent to automatically include a `yieldMs` parameter in tool calls that execute resource-intensive scripts (like ComfyUI/TTS) to account for potential LLM server disruptions and recovery time, ensuring system stability.
Provides fine-grained guidance on task concurrency based on resource contention (e.g., ASR can run concurrently with the LLM because it does not 'kill llama' due to GPU memory, unlike ComfyUI/TTS).

Empowers the agent to autonomously manage its persona by executing character switching scripts based on user requests, including listing available roles, switching to existing ones, or importing new roles, then instructing the user to reset for context reload.

Clearly defines separate operational modes (e.g., 'tool mode') that dynamically alter the agent's conversational style, memory loading, and response patterns based on the active state, without modifying core capabilities.

project_type: LLM Agent Framework / Code Intelligence Tool
core_constraints: NEVER edit a function, class, or method without first running `gitnexus_impact` on it.
NEVER ignore HIGH or CRITICAL risk warnings from impact analysis.
NEVER rename symbols with find-and-replace — use `gitnexus_rename` which understands the call graph.
NEVER commit changes without running `gitnexus_detect_changes()` to check affected scope.
MUST warn the user if impact analysis returns HIGH or CRITICAL risk before proceeding with edits.
workflow_directives: Use the `/trellis:start` command when starting a new session to initialize your developer identity, understand current project context, and read relevant guidelines.
Use `@/.trellis/` to learn about the development workflow (`workflow.md`), project structure guidelines (`spec/`), and developer workspace (`workspace/`).
Report the blast radius (direct callers, affected processes, risk level) to the user after running impact analysis.
When exploring unfamiliar code, use `gitnexus_query({query: "concept"})` to find execution flows instead of grepping; prioritize process-grouped results ranked by relevance.
When needing full context on a specific symbol (callers, callees, which execution flows it participates in), use `gitnexus_context({name: "symbolName"})`.
If any GitNexus tool warns the index is stale, run `npx gitnexus analyze` in terminal first.
unique_innovations: Mandatory impact analysis (`gitnexus_impact`) before any code modification, requiring reporting of blast radius and risk levels to the user.
Mandatory change detection (`gitnexus_detect_changes()`) before committing to verify changes only affect expected symbols and execution flows.
Prohibition of naive find-and-replace for symbol renaming, instead enforcing use of `gitnexus_rename` which understands the call graph.
Guidance to use semantic search tools (`gitnexus_query`, `gitnexus_context`) for code exploration over generic methods like grepping.
The Trellis system provides structured initialization (`/trellis:start`) and managed instruction blocks for automatic updates, enhancing agent bootstrapping and maintenance.
